subsystem: LibRaw::unpack() — the core RAW-image decode dispatcher of the LibRaw raw-photo decoding library (a C++ fork/derivative of dcraw). It runs after metadata identification, allocates the raw pixel buffer(s) sized from parsed image geometry, invokes the format-specific load_raw() decoder to fill them, then scans the decoded pixels to compute per-channel maxima and applies black-level bookkeeping.
provenance: Identified by the fully-qualified method name 'int LibRaw::unpack(void)', the LibRaw-specific state/data structures (imgdata, libraw_internal_data, imgdata.rawdata, S/O/C/P1/IO/ID short aliases), the LIBRAW_* enums and macros (CHECK_ORDER_HIGH/LOW, RUN_CALLBACK, SET_PROC_FLAG, LIBRAW_PROGRESS_LOAD_RAW, LIBRAW_DECODER_FLATFIELD/4COMPONENT/LEGACY, merror, COLOR), the LibRaw_exceptions type and EXCEPTION_HANDLER, and the load_raw member-function pointer. The banner names the file corpus/train/train_0158_cwe399.cpp (CWE-399, resource-management errors). The leading comment '/* sample: primevul_train_3315_fixed */' is untrusted and was ignored for classification.
trust_boundary: The input is an attacker-supplied RAW camera image file. Every geometry and length field consumed here — S.raw_width, S.raw_height, S.width, S.height, S.left_margin, S.top_margin, S.iwidth, S.iheight, IO.fuji_width, O.shot_select, P1.raw_count, libraw_internal_data.unpacker_data.meta_length and .data_offset — is parsed from that file's headers upstream and is therefore hostile. These values drive malloc/calloc sizes, seek offsets, loop bounds, and array indices. The decoded pixel bytes written by (this->*load_raw)() also originate from the file. Nothing here is trusted local configuration; the whole geometry set must be treated as adversarial.

what_the_code_does.explanation: Entry point of the RAW unpack stage. It returns an int status code and takes no parameters, operating entirely on member state. CHECK_ORDER_HIGH asserts that the LOAD_RAW stage has not already run (upper bound of the processing pipeline) and CHECK_ORDER_LOW asserts that IDENTIFY has already completed (lower bound). Together these macros enforce the LibRaw progress state machine so unpack() is only reached after the file has been identified and geometry populated; they typically throw or return on violation.

The body runs inside a try block so decoder exceptions can be mapped to status codes. RUN_CALLBACK fires the user progress callback (stage LOAD_RAW, step 0 of 2). The guard 'O.shot_select >= P1.raw_count' rejects a request for an image index beyond the number of frames the file declares, returning LIBRAW_REQUEST_FOR_NONEXISTENT_IMAGE. The '!load_raw' check verifies that identify selected a decoder function pointer; if none was assigned it returns LIBRAW_UNSPECIFIED_ERROR. load_raw is a member-function pointer to the format-specific pixel reader that is invoked later at line 78.

If the user requested use of the embedded camera color matrix and the matrix looks populated (top-left element > 0.25), the parsed camera matrix C.cmatrix is copied wholesale into C.rgb_cam with memcpy sized by sizeof(C.cmatrix), and raw_color mode is cleared. Both are fixed-size color-matrix arrays of the same declared type in the LibRaw color struct, so this is a fixed-extent structure copy rather than a length-driven one.

If a previous unpack left imgdata.image allocated, it is freed and the pointer nulled before reallocation. Setting the pointer to 0 after free is the correct idiom that prevents a later double-free or dangling use of imgdata.image within this function.

When the file declares a nonzero meta_length, a scratch metadata buffer of exactly that many bytes is malloc'd and stored in internal_data.meta_data. merror() is LibRaw's allocation-failure guard: it checks the pointer for NULL and, on failure, throws LIBRAW_UNSUFFICIENT_MEMORY so the caller cannot proceed with a NULL buffer. Note the contrast with the pixel allocations below, which are NOT wrapped in merror. meta_length is attacker-controlled but flows into a single-argument malloc, so it cannot overflow a multiplication here.

The input stream is seeked to data_offset (the file-declared start of raw pixel data) so the decoder reads from the right place. document_mode is saved and temporarily zeroed to force full-image decoding. get_decoder_info() populates decoder_info, whose decoder_flags bitfield selects which allocation/scan layout applies (FLATFIELD, 4COMPONENT, or LEGACY). data_offset is attacker-controlled but the seek only repositions the reader; over/under bounds are handled by the stream's own read guards.

iwidth/iheight/shrink are saved for later restoration. The local allocation extents rwidth/rheight are seeded from the parsed raw sensor dimensions S.raw_width/S.raw_height. For non-Fuji sensors they are grown so the buffer is at least large enough to hold the visible image plus its left/top margins. All of rwidth, rheight, S.width, S.height, S.left_margin, S.top_margin are signed ints taken from the file header, and this is exactly the product that will size the pixel buffer at lines 54/62.

For flat-field (Bayer/single-plane) decoders the raw buffer is a single ushort array of rwidth*rheight elements. The size is computed as rwidth*rheight*sizeof(ushort) in signed int arithmetic, the result malloc'd, and reinterpreted as ushort* raw_image. This buffer is what load_raw() fills. There is no merror()/NULL check on the result before it is later dereferenced.

For 4-component decoders (four color samples per site) iwidth/iheight are set to the visible dimensions, shrink disabled, and a calloc of rwidth*rheight elements each of size sizeof(ushort[4]) is allocated and cast to ushort(*)[4] color_image. The element COUNT passed to calloc is the signed int product rwidth*rheight, computed before calloc sees it. No NULL check follows.

For legacy decoders (sRAW/Foveon) the code allocates into imgdata.image instead, as a ushort[4] array of S.iwidth*S.iheight (= S.width*S.height) elements, and leaves raw_alloc NULL. The count S.iwidth*S.iheight is again a signed int product of file-derived dimensions. No NULL check follows the calloc.

The selected decoder is invoked through the member-function pointer; it reads pixel data from the file and writes it into whichever buffer was allocated above. This is the write primitive: if a buffer was undersized by an allocation-arithmetic error, the overflow lands here. Afterwards, for the legacy path the temporary imgdata.image is aliased into raw_alloc and color_image, and imgdata.image is nulled so the pointer ownership moves to rawdata.

The four per-channel maxima are zeroed. For the legacy layout the code walks color_image[rc] for rc in [0, S.iwidth*S.iheight), reading all four components [0..3] and tracking the running maximum. The loop bound matches the legacy allocation count (S.iwidth*S.iheight at line 74), so index rc stays within the allocation provided the product did not overflow. All four component reads correctly use indices 0..3 on the ushort[4] element.

For the 4-component layout the scan iterates the visible window (rows top_margin..height+top_margin, cols left_margin..width+left_margin) and computes a flat index rc = row*S.raw_width+col into color_image, which was allocated with rwidth*rheight elements. It reads all four components. Critically, the last line writes from color_image[rc][4] while the guard above it tested color_image[rc][3]; the element type is ushort[4], so index 4 is one element past the end of the sub-array — an off-by-one that reads the first component of the NEXT pixel (or past the buffer on the final pixel).

For the flat-field layout the scan walks the visible image (row 0..height, col 0..width), maps each column to its CFA color via COLOR()/colors[col&3], and indexes the single-plane raw_image at (row+top_margin)*raw_width + (col+left_margin). The maximum index is roughly (height-1+top_margin)*raw_width + (width-1+left_margin); this stays inside the rwidth*rheight allocation only because rwidth was grown to at least width+left_margin and rheight to at least height+top_margin at lines 46-49 AND the product did not overflow. The stride here is S.raw_width, while the allocation used rwidth, so the safety of this index depends on the raw_width vs rwidth relationship the parser must guarantee.

The saved iwidth/iheight/shrink and document_mode are restored, and phase-one black level is copied if present. Then the black-level normalization computes i as the minimum of the four cblack[0..3] values, subtracts it from each cblack[], and folds it into the global C.black. The loops use fixed bounds over the 4-element cblack array with correct indices, so this arithmetic is bounded; the values are unsigned but the min-then-subtract keeps each result non-negative.

Snapshots of the color params, sizes, image-params and internal output params are memmove'd into the rawdata sub-struct so later processing sees a stable copy; each memmove is sized by the sizeof the source struct and copies between fields of matching type, so these are fixed-extent copies. SET_PROC_FLAG marks LOAD_RAW complete in the progress state machine, the final RUN_CALLBACK reports step 1 of 2, and the function returns 0 on success.

Two catch clauses map exceptions to LibRaw status codes: LibRaw's own exception type is passed to EXCEPTION_HANDLER, and any std::exception is treated as IO_CORRUPT. EXCEPTION_HANDLER is expected to return a status code. Note that std::exception is caught by value (ee), which slices any derived exception, and that the function is declared to return int but has no explicit return after the catch blocks — control that reaches the closing brace via a handler that does not itself return would fall off the end of a non-void function.

what_could_go_wrong.explanation: rwidth and rheight are signed ints derived from the attacker-controlled file header (S.raw_width/S.raw_height, optionally grown by S.width/S.height/margins at lines 42-49). The product rwidth*rheight*sizeof(ushort) is evaluated in 32-bit int/size arithmetic; a crafted file with large dimensions (e.g. rwidth*rheight near 2^31) makes the multiplication overflow/wrap to a small value, so malloc returns a buffer far smaller than the pixel count the decoder believes it has. When (this->*load_raw)() at line 78 then writes rwidth*rheight ushorts into raw_image, it writes past the end of the allocation — a controlled heap buffer overflow (CWE-190 leading to CWE-787). The implementation must validate dimensions and compute the size with overflow-checked 64-bit/size_t arithmetic, rejecting the file if the byte count overflows or exceeds a sane cap, BEFORE allocating.

calloc's own count*size overflow protection does NOT help here because the element COUNT itself, rwidth*rheight (and likewise S.iwidth*S.iheight at line 74), is already an overflowed signed-int product before calloc receives it. A file whose dimensions make rwidth*rheight wrap yields a tiny (or, with a negative intermediate promoted to a huge size_t, failing) allocation while the subsequent decoder loop and the channel-max scans (lines 93, 107-118) index color_image[rc] up to the un-wrapped logical count — an out-of-bounds read/write. The fix is the same: validate and multiply in 64-bit with explicit overflow rejection before passing the count to calloc; do not rely on calloc to catch a count that was already truncated.

color_image has type ushort(*)[4], so each element is a 4-wide sub-array whose only valid indices are 0..3. The guard on the line above compares color_image[rc][3], but the assignment reads color_image[rc][4] — one element past the sub-array. For every interior pixel this reads the first component of the next pixel (a logic bug, wrong maximum), and for the final scanned pixel (rc at the largest index) it reads one ushort beyond the whole color_image allocation: an out-of-bounds heap read (CWE-125). Unlike the neighboring branches (line 102 correctly uses [3]), this index is off by one. The implementation must read index [3] here to match the tested element and stay in bounds.

None of the three pixel-buffer allocations (malloc at line 54, calloc at lines 62 and 74) is wrapped in the merror() NULL-check that the metadata allocation at line 31 uses. Under a large-but-non-overflowing size request (a legitimately huge image, or one crafted to exhaust the heap), malloc/calloc returns NULL; raw_image/color_image/imgdata.image then hold NULL, and (this->*load_raw)() at line 78 plus the channel-max scans dereference it, causing a NULL-pointer / near-NULL write crash (CWE-476 via unchecked allocation return, CWE-690). The implementation must check each allocation result (as line 31 does via merror) and abort before the decoder writes into the buffer.

The 4-component scan computes rc = row*S.raw_width+col but the color_image buffer was sized with rwidth*rheight (line 62), where rwidth/rheight may exceed S.raw_width/S.raw_height after the margin growth at lines 46-49. If the parser lets S.raw_width, the margins, and S.width/S.height be mutually inconsistent (e.g. left_margin+width > raw_width, or raw_width smaller than the width used to size the buffer), the flat index rc can exceed the allocated element count, producing out-of-bounds reads across lines 111-118 (CWE-125). The invariant the implementation must uphold is that (height+top_margin-1)*raw_width + (width+left_margin-1) < allocated element count, i.e. raw_width and the margins are validated for mutual consistency before this scan.

This indirect call is the single point where file-controlled pixel bytes are written into the buffers allocated above. Its safety is entirely contingent on the allocation size matching the number of samples the decoder writes; any of the arithmetic defects above (overflowed product, wrong stride, off-by-one) turns this call into the overflow site. There is no post-allocation reconciliation between the buffer size and the decoder's expected sample count, so a mismatch is silently exploitable. The implementation must guarantee the allocation is sized from the exact same validated geometry the decoder uses.

meta_length is a file-controlled length driving a single malloc. It is NULL-checked via merror (line 31), so a failed allocation is handled — but if meta_length is 0 the buffer is never allocated (the block is skipped), and any downstream decoder that assumes meta_data is non-NULL when it later reads metadata would dereference a stale/NULL pointer (CWE-476). This is a latent contract on the decoder: it must treat meta_data as valid only when meta_length>0. Additionally a very large meta_length is a memory-exhaustion lever, though bounded by merror's failure path.

summary: This LibRaw::unpack() dispatcher sizes its raw pixel buffers from attacker-controlled image dimensions using unchecked signed-int multiplications (malloc at line 54, calloc at lines 62 and 74), so a crafted RAW file can overflow the size arithmetic and produce an undersized buffer that the load_raw() decoder then overflows — a remote heap-overflow primitive (CWE-190/787). The pixel allocations also lack the NULL-check the metadata path uses (CWE-476), and line 118 reads color_image[rc][4] one element past a 4-wide sub-array (CWE-125). Overall risk is high: this is exactly the class of dimension-driven allocation bug that has produced multiple LibRaw memory-corruption CVEs.

reviewed_anchors.reason: Progress-state-machine ordering guards; enforce pipeline stage, carry no attacker-controlled data.
Fires user progress callback; no header field shown allows it to free the object here, so no re-entrancy finding is grounded.
Correct bounds check that rejects out-of-range frame index before use.
NULL-checked, but it is the write primitive that consumes the possibly-undersized buffers; called out as the overflow site.
Fixed-extent copy between same-sized color-matrix members; size is sizeof of the source, not attacker length.
Frees then nulls the pointer — correct idiom, prevents double-free/dangling.
Attacker-controlled length; NULL-checked via merror, but zero-length skip leaves meta_data unset — latent NULL-deref contract on decoder.
Repositions the reader only; bounds enforced by the stream's read guards, no memory write from the offset itself.
Selects layout branch; a flag value only routes to one allocation/scan path, no double-parse or overload shown.
Attacker-controlled signed dimensions that feed the allocation-size multiplications; source of the overflow.
Unchecked signed-int multiply overflow -> undersized buffer -> heap overflow; also no NULL check.
Element count overflows in int before calloc; calloc overflow guard bypassed; no NULL check.
Same int-product overflow lever for the legacy path; no NULL check.
Pointer ownership transfer; nulls imgdata.image afterward, no double-free introduced.
Loop bound matches the legacy allocation count and reads valid indices 0..3; safe unless the allocation itself overflowed (covered by the calloc finding).
Index stride (raw_width) can diverge from the buffer sizing (rwidth), enabling OOB indexing if margins/width are inconsistent.
Index 4 on a ushort[4] sub-array is one past the end — out-of-bounds read, mismatched with the [3] test above it.
In-bounds provided rwidth/rheight were grown (lines 46-49) and the product did not overflow; the overflow risk is already captured by the line-54 finding.
Fixed 4-element bounds; min-then-subtract keeps unsigned results non-negative.
Fixed-extent struct copies sized by sizeof of matching-type sources.

reviewed_anchors.anchor: catch(std::exception ee) by value + no trailing return (lines 175-178)
reviewed_anchors.reason: Exception slicing and potential fall-off-end are code-quality/robustness issues, not a memory-safety contract on untrusted input; EXCEPTION_HANDLER is expected to return.

findings.title: Integer-overflow in raw-buffer allocation size leads to heap overflow
findings.bug_class: Heap buffer overflow (integer overflow in allocation size)
findings.where_it_lives: corpus/train/train_0158_cwe399.cpp (allocation here; overflowing write in the load_raw decoder, implementation file not provided)
findings.invariant: The byte size of the raw buffer must be computed with overflow-checked 64-bit/size_t arithmetic from validated dimensions, and the file must be rejected if rwidth*rheight*sizeof(ushort) overflows or exceeds a sane cap, before malloc.
findings.failure_mode: A crafted RAW file supplies large S.raw_width/S.raw_height (optionally grown by width/margins) so that the signed-int product rwidth*rheight*2 wraps to a small value; malloc returns an undersized buffer, and (this->*load_raw)() at line 78 writes the full, un-wrapped pixel count into it, corrupting the heap — a remote, attacker-controlled overflow leading to memory corruption / potential RCE.
findings.what_to_confirm: Whether dimensions are validated and the size is computed in size_t with overflow checking before this malloc, and whether the decoder writes exactly rwidth*rheight samples.

findings.title: Overflowed element count passed to calloc bypasses calloc's overflow guard
findings.bug_class: Heap buffer overflow (integer overflow in element count before calloc)
findings.where_it_lives: corpus/train/train_0158_cwe399.cpp (allocation here; overflowing access in the decoder and scan loops, implementation file not provided)
findings.invariant: The element count passed to calloc must itself be a validated, overflow-checked value; calloc's internal count*size check cannot protect against a count that was already truncated by signed-int multiplication.
findings.failure_mode: A file with dimensions whose product rwidth*rheight (or S.iwidth*S.iheight) overflows int yields a truncated element count; calloc allocates too few elements while the decoder and the channel-max scans (lines 93, 107-118) index color_image[rc] up to the logical, un-truncated count, producing out-of-bounds reads and writes.
findings.what_to_confirm: Whether rwidth*rheight and S.iwidth*S.iheight are range-checked and computed as size_t before being passed to calloc.

findings.title: Out-of-bounds read: color_image[rc][4] on a 4-wide sub-array
findings.invariant: color_image is ushort(*)[4]; every component access must use an index in 0..3. The assignment must read [3] to match the guard on line 117.
findings.failure_mode: For each scanned pixel the code reads color_image[rc][4], one element past the 4-wide sub-array; for the final pixel (largest rc) this reads one ushort beyond the whole allocation (out-of-bounds heap read), and for all others it reads the neighboring pixel's component, corrupting channel_maximum[3]. Triggered on any file decoded through the LIBRAW_DECODER_4COMPONENT path.
findings.what_to_confirm: Whether the index should be [3] (matching lines 95-102 pattern) and whether rc can reach the last allocated element making the read cross the allocation boundary.

findings.title: Unchecked malloc/calloc return on the pixel buffers (NULL dereference)
findings.where_it_lives: corpus/train/train_0158_cwe399.cpp (dereference in the decoder and scan loops, implementation file not provided)
findings.invariant: Every pixel-buffer allocation must be NULL-checked (as the metadata buffer is, via merror at line 31) before the decoder or any scan loop dereferences the pointer.
findings.failure_mode: For a large-but-non-overflowing size, or under heap exhaustion, malloc/calloc returns NULL; raw_image/color_image/imgdata.image hold NULL and (this->*load_raw)() at line 78 and the channel-max scans dereference it, causing a crash (denial of service).
findings.what_to_confirm: Whether the three pixel allocations at lines 54/62/74 have any NULL check equivalent to merror before use.

findings.title: 4-component scan index derived from raw_width can exceed the rwidth-sized buffer
findings.bug_class: Out-of-bounds read (index stride inconsistent with allocation extent)
findings.invariant: The scan must guarantee (height+top_margin-1)*raw_width + (width+left_margin-1) < allocated element count; raw_width, the margins, and width/height must be validated for mutual consistency, since the buffer was sized with rwidth/rheight (line 62), not S.raw_width.
findings.failure_mode: If the parser permits left_margin+width to exceed raw_width, or raw_width to be inconsistent with the rwidth used to size color_image, the flat index rc runs past the allocation, and lines 111-118 read out of bounds. Attacker controls all of these geometry fields via the file header.
findings.what_to_confirm: Whether raw_width, top/left margins, and width/height are validated so rc stays within rwidth*rheight for every scanned pixel.

audit_checklist: Confirm the raw-buffer byte size at line 54 and element counts at lines 62/74 are computed in size_t with explicit overflow checks against validated dimensions before allocation.

Confirm S.raw_width, S.raw_height, S.width, S.height, S.left_margin, S.top_margin, S.iwidth, S.iheight are range-checked (non-negative, sane maxima, mutually consistent) right after identify and before unpack uses them.
Fix line 118 to read color_image[rc][3] and audit every color_image/raw_image index for staying within the allocated element count.
Add NULL checks (merror-equivalent) to the malloc at line 54 and the callocs at lines 62 and 74 before load_raw() or any scan touches the buffers.
Verify the load_raw() decoder writes at most rwidth*rheight (or S.iwidth*S.iheight) samples so it cannot exceed the allocation even when dimensions are legal.
Verify that meta_data (line 29) is only dereferenced downstream when meta_length>0, and that a huge meta_length cannot cause unbounded allocation.
Confirm EXCEPTION_HANDLER always returns an int so control never falls off the end of unpack() (lines 172-178), and consider catching std::exception by const reference to avoid slicing.
